These words from David are truly worthy of attention. Let those who have had a long experience of God’s goodness and the pleasantness of heavenly wisdom, when they come to the end of their lives, share what they know about the truth of the promise. David declares that he was inspired by God; the Spirit of God spoke through him. He and other holy people spoke and wrote as they were moved by the Holy Ghost. In many ways, he had his own neglect and wrong actions to blame. But David comforted himself with the thought that the Lord had made an everlasting covenant with him. By this, he mainly meant the covenant of mercy and peace that the Lord made with him as a sinner who believed in the promised Savior, embraced the promised blessing, and gave himself up to the Lord to be his redeemed servant. Believers will forever enjoy covenant blessings; and God the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost will forever be glorified in their salvation. Thus, forgiveness, righteousness, grace, and eternal life are secured as a gift from God through Jesus Christ. There is an infinite fullness of grace and all blessings stored up in Christ for those who seek his salvation. This covenant was everything to David; he knew so well the holy law of God and the extent of his own sinfulness that he understood what was needed for his own case in this salvation. It was therefore his greatest desire. Compared to this, all earthly things lost their appeal; he was willing to give them up or die and leave them behind so that he could experience full happiness. Still, the power of evil and the weakness of his faith, hope, and love were a grief and burden to him. He would have admitted that his own carelessness and lack of attention were the cause, but the hope that he would soon be made perfect in glory encouraged him in his dying moments.

David once earnestly longed for the water at the well of Bethlehem. It seems to be an example of weakness. He was thirsty; with the water from that well he had often refreshed himself when he was young, and it was without due thought that he desired it. Were his valiant men so eager to expose themselves at the slightest hint of their prince’s mind, and so willing to please him? Should we not long to approve ourselves to our Lord Jesus by readily complying with his will as shown us by his word, Spirit, and providence? But David poured out the water as a drink-offering to the Lord. In this way, he would go against his own foolish desire and punish himself for indulging it, and show that he had thoughtful consideration to correct his hasty actions, and knew how to deny himself. Did David view that water as very precious, which was obtained at the risk of these men’s lives? Should we not much more value those benefits for which our blessed Savior shed his blood? Let all beware of neglecting so great a salvation.
